patternDisplay: br cr dr fr gr pr tr
celebration: Leaf #9 — r-blends. Consonant plus /r/ run together (br · cr · dr · fr · gr · pr · tr). The high-frequency word 'from' unlocks this week.

songs.title: Fr-Fr-Frog!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
Fr! Fr! Fr-fr-fr!
(fr… fr… fr…)

[Hook]
Fr! Fr! Two sounds, TOGETHER!
/f/ and /r/ — fr! fr!
Fr-fr-frog! Fr-fr-frog!
FROG! (hop!) FROG!

[Verse 2]
Cr! Cr! /c/ and /r/!
Blend them quick — cr! cr!
Cr-cr-crab! Cr-cr-crab!
CRAB! (snip!) CRAB!

[Verse 3]
Dr! Dr! /d/ and /r/!
Blend them quick — dr! dr!
Dr-dr-drum! Dr-dr-drum!
DRUM! (bang!) DRUM!

[Verse 4]
Gr! Gr! /g/ and /r/!
Blend them quick — gr! gr!
Gr-gr-grin! Gr-gr-grin!
GRIN! (big grin!) GRIN!

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! No blend!
(fr… fr… fr…)

[Outro]
Fr-fr-frog! Cr-cr-crab!
Dr-dr-drum! Gr-gr-grin!
Two sounds together! (blend! blend!)
Fr! Fr! Fr-fr-fr!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: r-blends — a consonant plus /r/ run together (br, cr, dr, fr, gr, pr, tr), no vowel between ('fuh-rog' is the error). Good news for the EAL room: initial /r/ is NOT hard for Mandarin-L1 children (unlike final -l later), so these move at a normal pace. The /r/ is a soft growl with the tongue pulled back, not a tap. Never sing letter names.

songs.title: Frog Can Grin
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
Frog can grin!
Frog can grin!
A big, big grin! (grin! grin!)
Frog can grin!

[Verse 2]
Frog can bang the drum!
Crab can snap and grin!
Grin and grab and grin! (grin! grin!)
Frog can grin!

[Verse 3]
Snake can grin at Frog!
Chick can grin and clap!
Grin, Frog, grin! (grin! grin!)
Frog can grin!

[Outro]
Frog… the grin… (hush…)
A potato?! (POTATO!)
Frog can grin at a potato! (grin, Frog, grin!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'Frog can grin.' Every core word — frog, crab, drum, grin — appears, plus 'grab'. Verse 3 brings Snake and Chick into the grinning. Whisper the outro, potato hits loud, and even it gets a grin.

spreads.artNote: A plump green frog sitting in the spotlight, glossy-eyed, mouth a neutral line — waiting. A pond-lily under one foot.
The frog breaks into an enormous grin, mouth stretching wide across the whole face. Instant charm.
Extreme close-up of the grin — so wide it curls past the edges of the frog's face, every tooth showing, eyes crinkled. Pure comedy of scale.
The frog straddling a little drum, both webbed hands mid-bang, drumsticks blurred, still grinning. Beat-burst lines around the drum.
A round red crab scuttles in sideways, claws going snip-snip, a curious tilt to its eyestalks. New friend arrives.

spreads.text: Crab can grin from the grass.
spreads.artNote: The crab peeking out from a tuft of grass, wearing a grin of its own to match the frog's. Two grinners now.
Snake — emerald-green with large gentle amber eyes — nose to nose with the frog, both grinning enormously at each other. Grin contagion.
The whole gang — Frog, Crab, Snake, Chick, Sheep — lined up in a row, every single one grinning ear to ear, the drum in front. A wall of joy.
A single potato sits in the tuft of grass wearing a drawn-on grin exactly like the frog's, out-grinning them all. The cast stares, grins frozen. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it.

sentences: Crab can grin from the grass.
extras: PATTERN TREE: leaf #9 — r-blends. Read frog/crab/drum/grin aloud and hang it.
NO-SCHWA MODELING: br/cr/dr/fr/gr/pr/tr are single quick moves. The /r/ is a soft back-of-mouth growl, not a tap.
EAL note: initial /r/ is easy for Mandarin-L1 children — move at full speed here; save the extra time for the hard final blends coming in Weeks 36–37.

'FROM' unlocks — a small, hugely common word, now fully decodable (f-r-o-m). Add it to the pocket-chart sentence bank. Moveable alphabet: build the r-blend as a pair first (fr, cr, dr, gr) then finish the word.

teacherFocus: Third blend week — r-blends (br, cr, dr, fr, gr, pr, tr). No schwa: 'fr' is one quick move. The /r/ is a soft growl with the tongue pulled back. This is the easy blend for your EAL room — initial /r/ is not a Mandarin-L1 problem, so run these at full speed and bank the saved energy for the genuinely hard final blends in Weeks 36 and 37. Celebrate 'from' unlocking — a tiny word the class meets everywhere, now fully theirs. Hang leaf #9; the blend branch is nearly full.
